meta.coverage_notes: 45 competitors. APAC-weighted = 18/45 = 40% (AU 9, JP 4, HK 2, SG 2, KR/Canada-split 1). Australia anchors the APAC slice with 9 records — consistent with Lumana's primary market. Aged-care vertical is thin in pure SG/HK pure-plays, so APAC-broad weighting (AU + JP + HK + SG + KR) was used per agent brief. Category mix: global_incumbent 18, regional_challenger 9, sg_local 11 (the 'local-to-our-target-market' bucket — AU/HK/SG players), adjacent 4, diy_alternative 3. Modality split: ambient-sensing (radar/mmWave/thermal) 12; vision (camera + AI) 4; wearables/PERS 5; nurse-call + sensor pads 5; care-home OS / EMR / care-plan workflow 6; therapeutic + DIY 4. Source list: safely-you.com, nobi.life, vayyar.com, xkcorp.com, virtusense.ai, carepredict.com, keplervision.eu, austco.com, tunstall.com, tunstallhealthcare.com.au, autumn.care, leecare.com.au, personcentredsoftware.com, parorobots.com, konicaminolta.com (HitomeQ Care Support), MobiHealthNews (Fujitsu mmWave 2024 trial), HKBN gerontech press release 2025-07, SCMP HK fall-detection coverage, ariia.org.au, talius.com.au, heidihealth.com, werkdone.com, pulsesync.com, Crunchbase funding pages, LinkedIn company pages.
meta.handoff_to_agent_2: Top-5 threat vectors: (1) Nurse-call pad incumbents (Austco/Inovonics/Tunstall) own the operator's existing wiring closet — wedge through workflow + auto-doc, not pad replacement. (2) Vayyar/Kardian sell radar as a component, not a workflow — Lumana's productisation gap is real. (3) SafelyYou + Nobi own the privacy-compliant mindshare conversation in US/EU but neither has AU residential RAC depth. (4) Care-home OS suites (PCS/AutumnCare/Leecare) own EMR; we must integrate, not replace. (5) The dominant NBA is 'do nothing + hire agency' — every market-sizing model must explicitly carve this out.

field_notes.aud_monthly_per_bed: Per-bed monthly AUD estimate, midpoint of pricing_range_published, converted at FX: USD→1.55, EUR→1.65, GBP→1.95, SGD→1.15, HKD→0.20, JPY→0.01. null where pricing is capex-only, per-home consumer, or otherwise not per-bed comparable.

top_five.rationale: The dominant NBA. Every operator's default is 'hire more agency RNs' — and the labour shortage (110k by 2030) is the structural failure mode Lumana's ROI story must close in 90 days.
Privacy-passing lamp form-factor + AU distributor signed; the only foreign incumbent that can credibly enter AU residential aged care without a Royal-Commission-grade privacy fight.
ASX-listed nurse-call incumbent already wired into 12,000+ bedrooms; they don't need to win a deal — they only need to ship an ambient-sensing SKU on top of installed Tacera infrastructure.
450+ US senior-living deployments + published JAMDA fall-reduction RCT; if they translate the camera framing into AU + HK, the clinical evidence base outpaces every challenger including us.
USD 300M+ on 4D-imaging radar sold as chip + SDK to every integrator (Eevi, K4Connect, Alexa); they own the radar narrative every ambient startup including Lumana competes against.

competitors.name: SafelyYou (Halo)
competitors.pricing_range_published: Hidden — bundled per community; press indicates ~USD 50–80/bed/mo equivalent
competitors.primary_value_prop: AI camera + 24/7 human review that confirms falls and feeds clinical workflow.
strengths: 450+ US communities deployed (per safely-you.com 2026)
Published JAMDA / AJMC fall-reduction RCT — 40% fewer falls
USD 100M+ raised; 80% of largest US senior-living operators are customers (company claim 2025)
weaknesses: Camera-in-bedroom is a near-instant disqualifier in AU Royal Commission and HK family contexts
Human-review centre is a US-time-zone cost base; APAC service window is unproven
No published AU/HK/SG residential aged-care deployments as of 2026-04

website_design_notes.verdict: Confident clinical-tech aesthetic with a dark-teal hero, custom product photography of the bedroom AI camera, and a JAMDA RCT stat hammered above the fold. Pricing is hidden behind 'Request demo' which caps coherence; the camera-in-bedroom imagery confronts the privacy objection head-on rather than hiding it. Screenshots pending — placeholder path retained.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: Lead the hero with a single quantified clinical outcome ('40% fewer falls, JAMDA-published') instead of a feature list — proof beats promise.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not hide pricing behind a sales gate; AU operators on PSG-equivalent grant cycles will not 'request a demo' to learn if you fit their per-bed budget.

competitors.name: Oxehealth (LIO)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Optical infrared sensor with vitals tracking for safety and patient observation.
strengths: Deployed across ~50% of NHS mental health trusts (per company About page 2025)
Class-IIa MDR medical-device regulatory clearance in UK
USD ~22M raised; Oxford Sciences Innovation backed
weaknesses: Positioned for psych/secure-care, not residential aged care
Sensor still video-class — privacy framing weaker than radar in AU debate
No APAC commercial footprint

website_design_notes.verdict: LIO Health's site is the cleanest clinical-evidence story in the set: hero leads with 'Vital signs without contact', then immediately surfaces the NHS deployment count and peer-reviewed citations. Custom product photography of a vision sensor in a clinical room, restrained palette (deep blue + white + one accent), single 'Book a clinical consultation' CTA. Pricing is enterprise-routed but coherence holds because the buyer journey is unambiguous from hero scroll-1.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: The 'evidence first, sales second' scroll order — citation count, regulator approvals, and named NHS trusts appear before any feature list. For Lumana selling into Royal-Commission-anxious operators, evidence-first is the only credible order.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not adopt LIO's hospital/acute-care visual cues (vital-signs waveform graphics) — Lumana sells into residential aged care where clinical-monitor imagery raises the wrong objection ('we're not a hospital').

competitors.name: Nobi Smart Lamps
competitors.pricing_range_published: EUR 1,200–1,800 per lamp + monthly service (per EU dealer pages 2025)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Ceiling smart-lamp with on-edge AI fall detection; lamp form-factor avoids camera framing.
strengths: EUR 48M+ raised; 22+ country distribution including AU/NZ (per nobi.life About 2026)
Lamp form-factor wins the privacy framing battle vs. cameras
Active AU distributor signed — Welldercare and others
weaknesses: Per-lamp capex (~EUR 1.5k) is high vs. radar wall-plate alternatives
Workflow stops at the alert — no auto-documentation back into care plan
AU residential aged-care customer count not yet disclosed

website_design_notes.verdict: Best-in-class hero: the lamp itself is the visual brand, photographed in a real bedroom with warm light and zero stock-photo cliches. Product-as-hero, soft-typography, single accent colour — every page reinforces 'dignified, privacy-first ambient'. Demo CTA is single and unambiguous; pricing is the only weak link (partner-routed).
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: Make the device the hero. A real-context photo of the actual sensor in a real resident bedroom out-trusts any abstract 'AI' illustration Lumana could commission.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not adopt Nobi's all-lowercase wordmark and ultra-minimal nav — it works for a single-SKU lamp but would obscure Lumana's multi-stakeholder (operator, RN, family) journeys.

competitors.name: Vayyar Care
competitors.pricing_range_published: Hardware ~USD 400–700 per device + integrator-set service; not published direct
competitors.primary_value_prop: 4D-imaging radar chip + SDK sold to senior-care integrators for fall and presence detection.
strengths: USD 300M+ raised across multiple rounds (Crunchbase 2025)
Reference design used by K4Connect, Amazon Alexa Together partners
Privacy-by-design narrative (no imagery) wins against cameras
weaknesses: Component-only — no end-to-end workflow or documentation
Buyer is the integrator, not the RAC operator — slow GTM into aged care direct
Per-room hardware cost is the price floor every reseller fights

website_design_notes.verdict: Polished startup site with a strong '4D radar' hero render and clean type, but Coherence caps at 5: the same homepage tries to sell the chip-and-SDK to integrators, the device to senior-living, and the auto sensor to OEMs — three buyers, one page, no clear pivot. Pricing absent (component sale). Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: The radar visualisation in the hero — an animated point-cloud showing 'see without seeing' — is exactly the kind of trust-building proof asset Lumana should commission for its own ambient-sensing pitch.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not stack three distinct buyer personas on one homepage. Lumana sells to operators; route the radar-curious integrator to a separate /developers page and keep the front door for procurement and DON personas only.

competitors.name: Xandar Kardian (XK300)
competitors.pricing_range_published: USD 600–900 device + USD 25–40/bed/mo SaaS (per US channel quotes 2025)
competitors.primary_value_prop: FDA 510(k)-cleared UWB impulse radar for continuous contactless vitals.
strengths: FDA 510(k) clearance is the strongest regulatory posture in the radar category
Korean parent + Canadian commercial = APAC-credible narrative
50+ facility deployments per company materials 2025
weaknesses: Vitals-first framing isn't what RAC operators pay for — fall workflow is
No AU TGA equivalent listing as of 2026-04
Single-room device economics; cost-per-bed sits above the $85 anchor

competitors.name: VirtuSense VSTAlert
competitors.primary_value_prop: LIDAR + IR spatial AI predicts bed-exits 30–65 seconds before they happen.
strengths: USD ~90M raised across rounds (Crunchbase 2025)
Pre-fall prediction window is a differentiator no AU competitor matches
Hundreds of US LTC organisations deployed (company materials 2025)
weaknesses: LIDAR price floor pushes per-bed cost above operator tolerance
US-only deployments; no APAC reference sites
Predictive promise creates false-positive complaints in 1–3 star G2 reviews

competitors.name: CarePredict (Tempo)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Wrist-worn AI wearable plus location beacons predicting ADL decline.
strengths: USD ~29M raised (Crunchbase 2025)
Published predictive-analytics case studies on UTI and falls
Multi-national senior-living chain references
weaknesses: Wearable compliance failure: ~80% of residents stop wearing the band — same dynamic Lumana cites
Per-resident cost stack is wearable + beacon + service — heavy
Battery + skin-irritation reviews recurring on G2

competitors.name: Kepler Vision (Night Nurse)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Camera + AI that detects falls without storing video, marketed as Night Nurse.
strengths: EUR ~6M raised; UvA computer-vision spinout (Crunchbase 2024)
No-replay architecture is the strongest privacy framing for a vision system
NL/BE customer references
weaknesses: Still a camera; AU regulator + family reaction does not parse 'no replay'
Small team, limited APAC sales bandwidth
Single-modality — vision only

website_design_notes.verdict: Dutch CV-research-spinout site: tech-forward dark hero, neural-network animation, but the 'Night Nurse' product is buried under three layers of academic positioning (technology platform > use cases > products). UI Clarity caps at 5 — pricing is absent, demo lives in a footer-only contact form, and the nav 'Solutions / Technology / About' is marketing-speak. Otherwise visually competent.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: The product naming discipline — 'Night Nurse' is a single, memorable, role-mapped product name. Lumana should consider naming the product after the night-shift role it relieves rather than describing the technology.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not let the technology platform narrative (neural networks, computer vision research) lead the homepage. Operators buy outcomes; the CV detail belongs on /technology, not above the fold.

competitors.name: Tellus You Care
competitors.primary_value_prop: Ambient RF sensors detecting movement and falls without cameras or wearables.
strengths: Privacy-first RF positioning aligned with aged-care regulator preference
Light footprint hardware (single hub per home)
Y Combinator alumnus, A2 pilot stage
weaknesses: Aimed at home-aging market, not residential aged-care operators
Pre-revenue against the operator buyer; no facility deployments
Small team (<15 LinkedIn) — APAC entry implausible in 12 months

competitors.name: Essence SmartCare
competitors.pricing_range_published: Sold via service providers; end-user retail SGD 30–60/mo
competitors.primary_value_prop: Multi-sensor home telecare hub with PERS, motion, and mmWave radar add-ons.
strengths: Listed parent (TASE: ESSC) with global telecare distribution
Multi-modality — PERS + radar + motion under one roof
Channel partners in AU and SG (telcos / utilities)
weaknesses: Telecare-shaped — not residential aged-care workflow
White-label motion means no operator brand recall
PERS pendant compliance failure repeats CarePredict's pattern

competitors.name: Eevi
competitors.primary_value_prop: Australian aged-care integrator using Vayyar radar plus a thin workflow layer.
strengths: Brisbane HQ; only AU-incorporated radar player at any scale
Direct AU RAC references (per partner case studies 2024–25)
Bootstrapped, lean operating model
weaknesses: Radar comes from Vayyar — no upstream defensibility
Workflow stops at alert; documentation handed back to staff manually
No published clinical study; Vayyar's accuracy claims travel with them

competitors.name: Austco Tacera
competitors.pricing_range_published: Capex per bed (AUD 800–2,000) + service contract; not published
competitors.primary_value_prop: Nurse-call platform with sensor and pad integrations for bedside alerting.
strengths: 12,000+ facilities globally per Austco corporate fact sheet 2024
ASX-listed parent (Azure Healthcare ASX:AZV); AU-native sales channel
Already wired into the bedroom — install advantage no startup matches
weaknesses: 52% pad false-positive rate is the alert-fatigue evidence Lumana uses against them
Pad / call-bell paradigm is decades old; no ambient sensing native
Slow software roadmap — last major Tacera UI refresh 2023

website_design_notes.verdict: ASX-incumbent corporate site: blue-grey palette, stock hospital photography, dense product/solution mega-nav typical of B2B hardware vendors circa 2019. Coherence is fine — they sell nurse-call, the site looks like a nurse-call vendor — but Modernness lags and CTA hierarchy is weak (three competing 'Contact / Brochure / Partner Login' links). Accessibility note: low-contrast grey-on-white secondary text, common in this template family. Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: The product/solution split nav is functionally clear for a procurement officer who already knows what they want — Lumana's nav should preserve a similar 'Product | Outcomes | Integrations | Pricing' clarity.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not inherit the stock-hospital-photography habit (smiling nurse-with-tablet shots). Lumana's brand promise is 'reliability', and stock imagery undermines it more than any 2019 typography choice.

competitors.name: K4Connect
competitors.primary_value_prop: Senior-living operating system bundling smart-home, sensors, and engagement.
strengths: USD ~44M raised (Crunchbase 2025)
300+ communities deployed
Suite breadth — engagement + monitoring under one contract
weaknesses: Independent + assisted-living focus, not high-acuity residential aged care
Sensors via Vayyar OEM — same component-vendor dependence as Eevi
US-only; no APAC presence

competitors.name: Caregility
competitors.pricing_range_published: Enterprise SaaS — USD 60–120/bed/mo equivalent (per HIMSS coverage 2024)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Hospital-grade virtual care platform with bedside cameras and ambient monitoring.
strengths: USD ~100M raised (Crunchbase 2024)
1,200+ hospital deployments
Strong virtual nursing GTM
weaknesses: Hospital ICP — translates poorly to residential aged-care economics
Camera-centric — fails the same AU privacy test
Per-bed price floor too high for AU $85 anchor

competitors.name: Tunstall Healthcare
competitors.primary_value_prop: Pan-European telecare and PERS leader bundling pendants, pull-cords, and pads.
strengths: 5M+ users globally; PE-backed (Charterhouse) per Tunstall 2024 disclosures
Established AU + SG + HK telecare contracts
End-to-end including monitored response centres
weaknesses: PERS-pendant DNA — Lumana's anti-wearable thesis attacks them directly
Pads are exactly the alert-fatigue source RAC operators want to retire
Response-centre cost base is a structural margin trap

competitors.name: GrandCare Systems
competitors.primary_value_prop: Tablet plus motion + door sensors for remote caregiver oversight at home.
strengths: Bootstrapped and stable since 2005
Touchscreen UX in-room is a differentiator for home-care
Public pricing — rare in the category
weaknesses: Home-care, not residential aged-care
No mobile-first product; tablet-era hardware
Tiny commercial footprint outside US

competitors.name: Lively (Best Buy Health)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Consumer mobile PERS pendants and Jitterbug phones with urgent-response.
strengths: Best Buy Health distribution (acquired 2018)
Mass-consumer brand recall
Public retail pricing
weaknesses: Pendant compliance failure — same anti-wearable thesis applies
Consumer ICP, not operator buyer
US-only retail footprint

competitors.name: Alarm.com Wellness
competitors.pricing_range_published: Dealer-set; consumer USD 30–60/mo bundled with smart-home
competitors.primary_value_prop: Smart-home wellness add-on bundling motion, voice, and contact sensors.
strengths: NASDAQ:ALRM scale; established AU dealer channel
Distribution moat through 9,000+ smart-home dealers
Cross-sell into existing security accounts
weaknesses: Smart-home shaped — never sold into residential aged-care operator buyer
No clinical workflow
Sensor stack is consumer-grade, not facility-grade

competitors.name: Cherish Health
competitors.primary_value_prop: Wall-plate radar (Cherish Serenity) for fall and motion detection.
strengths: USD ~15M raised (Crunchbase 2024)
Wall-plate form factor competes directly with Vayyar / Kardian
Discharge-to-home angle differentiates
weaknesses: Early commercial; deployment count not public
US-only
Single-modality (radar) — no workflow layer

competitors.name: Anthropos
competitors.primary_value_prop: Sensors plus a care-circle app for UK domiciliary care.
strengths: GBP ~4M raised; UK home-care pilots active
Sensor + app + family loop is well thought-through
Public consumer pricing
weaknesses: Domiciliary, not residential — wrong buyer for Lumana
Single market (UK)
Tiny LinkedIn headcount (<20) — APAC entry implausible

competitors.name: Amazon Alexa Together
competitors.primary_value_prop: Echo + Vayyar partner radar for consumer aging-in-place monitoring.
strengths: Amazon distribution + brand
Vayyar OEM gives radar credibility
Public subscription pricing
weaknesses: Consumer service — not sold into RAC operator
Discontinued discussions in US press 2024 — sustained commitment unclear
Privacy + Echo always-on is a non-starter in AU RAC contexts

competitors.name: Inovonics
competitors.primary_value_prop: Wireless sensor network powering nurse-call and pendant infrastructure.
strengths: 8,000+ facilities deployed (Inovonics corp 2024)
Privately held since 1986; mature channel
Open protocol used by many integrators
weaknesses: Pendant + pad architecture is the alert-fatigue legacy Lumana attacks
No ambient sensing, no auto-doc
US-only commercial reach

competitors.name: Sensio
competitors.primary_value_prop: Nordic smart-care platform integrating sensors, nurse-call, and EHR.
strengths: EUR ~30M raised; Nordic market leader
Integration-platform DNA — sells to operator IT, not to clinicians
Public-procurement track record
weaknesses: Nordic-language tooling; APAC localisation absent
Long enterprise sales cycle
Sensor agnostic — no proprietary edge

competitors.name: Tynetec / Oku (Legrand)
competitors.primary_value_prop: UK telecare hardware portfolio under Legrand Care.
strengths: Legrand parent (EPA:LR) provides scale
Deep council-procurement relationships
Hardware reliability track record
weaknesses: Hardware-only; software is thin
Pad-DNA again — alert-fatigue baked in
No APAC presence

competitors.name: Fujitsu mmWave Care
competitors.pricing_range_published: Pilot pricing only — not yet commercial as of 2026
competitors.primary_value_prop: Fujitsu + AI mmWave radar trial for fall detection in Japanese aged care.
strengths: Fujitsu balance sheet + Japanese government relationships
Active 2024–25 pilot announced via MobiHealthNews
Privacy framing aligned with JP regulator preference
weaknesses: Trial-stage — no commercial SKU
Slow-moving incumbent — pilot to product cycles measured in years
JP-only; export plan unclear

competitors.name: Finggal Link
competitors.primary_value_prop: Japanese radar + IR sensor OEM for elderly-care platforms.
strengths: Established 1990; deep JP institutional trust
JP nursing-home customer base
Local-language support
weaknesses: OEM-component model — same upstream-only weakness as Vayyar
JP-only commercial footprint
No workflow / documentation layer

competitors.name: PARO Therapeutic Robot
competitors.primary_value_prop: Therapeutic seal robot with 5 sensors used to calm dementia patients.
strengths: AIST-developed; 30+ years of clinical evidence
Class II medical device (US)
Recurring AU + JP dementia-care purchases
weaknesses: Therapy device — not safety/monitoring; adjacent at best
Capex per unit; no workflow integration
Single-purpose product

website_design_notes.verdict: Frozen-in-2010 product site: small body type, table-based layouts, low-resolution product photos of the seal robot, multiple competing CTAs ('Order PARO', 'Find Distributor', 'Research'). Brand-service mismatch — the product is a clinically-validated dementia therapy tool, but the site reads like an eBay listing. Coherence drops to 4 because pricing is buried and the demo path is 'email a distributor'. Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: The decades of clinical research links in the footer are an underrated trust signal — Lumana should mirror this with a 'Published evidence' permanent footer link the moment its first peer-reviewed pilot lands.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Reject everything else. PARO is the cautionary case for what happens when a clinically-validated product is fronted by a site that signals 'unmaintained' — the validation is real but the brand reads as defunct.

competitors.name: HKBN Gerontech Safety Bell
competitors.primary_value_prop: Wireless emergency safety bell with fall detection bundled with HKBN home internet.
strengths: HKBN consumer + telco distribution in HK
First HK-launched gerontech product (per HKBN release 2025-07)
Public retail pricing in HKD
weaknesses: Consumer-shaped — not sold into HK residential elderly homes
Single device; no platform
Telco partnership exposure — discontinuation risk

website_design_notes.verdict: Telco-portal pattern: HKBN's gerontech safety bell sits inside a sprawling consumer telco site (broadband, mobile, enterprise, gerontech all in one mega-nav). UI Clarity caps at 3 — finding the safety-bell product page from the homepage takes 4+ clicks through marketing-speak categories. Bilingual EN/ZH toggle is a positive signal for the HK market but switches break some sub-pages. Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: Bilingual toggle (EN/ZH) is non-negotiable for any HK go-to-market — Lumana should bake i18n in from day one rather than retrofit it for the HKBN-style B2B2C channel deals.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Do not bury the aged-care product inside a multi-product corporate site. If Lumana ever sells through a telco channel partner, insist on a co-branded standalone microsite, not a deep-link inside the partner's mega-nav.

competitors.name: Guardforce Smart Care
competitors.pricing_range_published: Hidden — bundled with security service contracts
competitors.primary_value_prop: Anti-wandering and panic-button systems bundled with security monitoring.
strengths: Established HK security distribution + 24/7 monitoring centre
RCHE references in HK
HK-localised Cantonese service
weaknesses: Wearable bracelet — same compliance issue Lumana cites
Hardware shaped — no clinical workflow
HK-only

competitors.name: Person Centred Software (mCare)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Mobile clinical care system, eMAR, and care-plan workflow for residential aged care.
strengths: 100,000+ residents on platform globally; 3,590+ care homes since 2015 (PCS 2024)
AU entity launched 2016; AU references include large chains
Owns the operator workflow Lumana's documentation must integrate with
weaknesses: Not sensing — only documents what staff manually enter
Slow ML/AI roadmap
Per-bed pricing leaves little SaaS budget headroom for sensing layer

competitors.name: AutumnCare
competitors.primary_value_prop: Australian clinical aged-care management software (Desktop + Cloud).
strengths: 15+ years AU residential aged-care vertical focus
AU-based data centres — meets Aged Care Quality Standards
Strong AN-ACC compliance positioning
weaknesses: Documentation only — no sensing or alerting layer
Desktop-era UX still exists in many sites
Slow integration roadmap with sensor vendors

competitors.name: Leecare Solutions (Platinum6)
competitors.primary_value_prop: End-to-end clinical EHR for aged-care continuum on the Platinum6 platform.
strengths: AU-founded; deep regulator + funding rule familiarity
References in SG + HK aged-care operators
Single-record continuum is rare in the category
weaknesses: Heavy implementation cycle (6–12 months typical)
No sensing layer; relies on staff entry
Per-bed pricing competes with PCS for the same line on the operator P&L

competitors.name: Epicor Senior Living Solution / iQX
competitors.pricing_range_published: Enterprise pricing — not published; AUD ~20–35/bed/mo equivalent
competitors.primary_value_prop: Enterprise senior-living management with finance, HR, and clinical modules.
strengths: Global ERP-grade reliability
AU senior-living chain references
Wins where the operator already runs Epicor finance
weaknesses: Heavy enterprise software — slow operator change cycles
No sensing or fall workflow
Fights the same line on the budget as PCS / Leecare

competitors.name: DIY: Do Nothing + Hire Agency Staff
competitors.pricing_range_published: AUD ~180k/year per added RN (per Lumana admin-legacy NBA table)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Keep status quo. Hire more agency RNs / PCAs to cover staffing minutes.
strengths: Zero switching cost; familiar to operator and staff
Doesn't trigger family / regulator privacy debate
No procurement cycle
weaknesses: AU forecasts 110k aged-care worker shortfall by 2030 — labour solution is structurally failing
Agency rates in AU rose 30–60% 2022–24
Documentation hours (~13.5/wk per RN) are the cost Lumana directly recovers

website_design_notes.verdict: Australian Government health.gov.au aged-care portal: standard AGOV design system (red banner, sans body, dense link lists). Coherence is acceptable for a portal but UI Clarity suffers under a four-level breadcrumb taxonomy that buries operator-facing pages under consumer ones. Accessibility is the rubric leader here — the AGOV system enforces WCAG AA contrast and keyboard nav. Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: Steal the AGOV accessibility discipline: WCAG AA contrast on every CTA, visible focus states, and 200% zoom that doesn't break the layout — table stakes for selling into government-funded aged-care providers.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Reject the portal-style information architecture. A government portal optimises for 'find any one of 10,000 pages'; a Lumana commercial site must optimise for 'three buyer intents in 10 seconds'.

competitors.name: DIY: Existing Nurse-Call + Bed/Chair Pads
competitors.pricing_range_published: AUD ~30k/year capex amortised + pad consumables (per Lumana NBA table)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Use the call-bell, bed-exit pad, and chair-exit pad already in the room.
strengths: Already paid for; sunk-capex psychology
No vendor procurement
Familiar to staff
weaknesses: 52% false-positive rate (cited field studies) — alert fatigue
Pads do not document the event
Pad replacement consumables are a hidden recurring cost

competitors.name: DIY: Build It In-House
target_market: Top-3 AU listed chains (Opal, Regis, Estia)
competitors.pricing_range_published: AUD 500k+/year fully loaded (per Lumana NBA table)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Stand up an internal data + product team to build sensing + workflow.
strengths: Full IP control; data stays inside the operator
Aligns to operator's existing systems
No vendor lock-in
weaknesses: 1–2 large chains have tried; none shipped to clinical-validation grade
Hardware procurement + clinical trial knowledge does not exist in-house
Recurring engineering cost without per-bed amortisation

competitors.name: Heidi Health (clinical AI scribe)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Voice-to-note clinical AI scribe used by clinicians during consults.
strengths: AUD ~30M raised; strong AU clinician traction
AU-built, AU data residency
Lowest-friction documentation tool in AU primary care 2025
weaknesses: Records what the clinician says — still relies on staff being present and remembering the event
GP-shaped UX; needs adaptation for RAC PCAs
Per-clinician pricing scales poorly across a 90-bed RAC roster

competitors.name: AltumView (Sentinare)
competitors.pricing_range_published: USD 299 device + USD 5–15/mo per device (per altumview.ca 2025)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Camera that converts to a stick-figure on-device for privacy-friendly fall detection.
strengths: Stick-figure abstraction is a credible privacy framing
Public consumer pricing — anchors low end of market
Active AU consumer distribution
weaknesses: Still a camera at point-of-capture — fails strict AU regulator interpretations
Consumer-shaped — not RAC operator workflow
Tiny brand and team

competitors.name: Billy
competitors.pricing_range_published: AUD 35–70/mo per home (per Billy product pages 2025)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Discreet motion + door + temp sensors for older Australians staying at home.
strengths: AU-founded, AU customer base in home-care segment
Sensor stack genuinely passive — no wearable, no camera
Public consumer pricing
weaknesses: Aimed at home, not RAC; operator buyer is foreign to them
Behavioural inference, not real-time fall detection
Small team — APAC RAC entry implausible in 12 months

competitors.name: Smarter Safer Homes (CSIRO)
competitors.pricing_range_published: Grant-funded pilots; not commercial as of 2026
competitors.primary_value_prop: CSIRO-developed sensor system for older people at home, with remote family + carer access.
strengths: CSIRO brand is the strongest research credibility signal in AU healthtech
Long pilot track-record across multiple AU states
weaknesses: Research-stage; no operator-facing commercial SKU
Home-care framing, not RAC
Slow technology-transfer pipeline

competitors.name: Konica Minolta HitomeQ Care Support
competitors.pricing_range_published: Subscription since 2021 — JPY ~5,000–10,000 per bed / mo (per Konica Minolta corp materials 2024)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Ceiling sensor + 24GHz microwave + behavioural AI for JP nursing-home staff workflow.
strengths: Konica Minolta corporate balance sheet + sensor manufacturing depth
Marubeni co-investment (2022) signals scale ambition
Subscription model since 2021 — proven JP RAC commercial traction
weaknesses: JP-only commercial footprint; no AU/HK/SG channel
Image-sensing heritage triggers privacy questions outside JP
Behavioural baselines need 30+ day calibration per resident

competitors.name: Tunstall Healthcare Australasia
competitors.pricing_range_published: AUD 39–69/mo bundled with monitoring; capex hidden (per tunstallhealthcare.com.au 2026)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Connected-care + 24/7 Brisbane response centre with pendants, smart watches, sensors.
strengths: 45,000+ AU clients (per Tunstall AU corporate page 2025)
Operates AU response centre — local response time + privacy posture
Aged Care Royal Commission-aligned positioning + grant-funded SKUs
weaknesses: PERS pendant compliance failure inherited from group
Pad + pull-cord legacy stack
Response-centre cost base under margin pressure

competitors.name: Talius Group (Sensor + Closure)
competitors.pricing_range_published: Capex per facility AUD 40–120k + service contract; not published
competitors.primary_value_prop: AU-listed sensor + smart-closure provider for aged-care and retirement villages.
strengths: ASX:TAL listing — operator IT trusts AU-listed counterparties
AU-only focus — no distraction across markets
Distribution into existing retirement-village + RAC accounts
weaknesses: Hardware-DNA — software is thin
Pad-class sensors: same false-positive issue Lumana attacks
Listed micro-cap — limited R&D headroom

competitors.name: PulseSync (SG eldercare innovation)
competitors.pricing_range_published: Hidden — bundled with implementation; SG channel reports SGD 30–60/bed/mo
competitors.primary_value_prop: Singapore-built eldercare platform aggregating sensor + workflow signals for nursing homes.
strengths: Singapore-based; aligned to AIC + MOH expectations
PDPA + local data residency posture
SG nursing-home references in early commercial pilots
weaknesses: Small team and capital base
Sensor-agnostic — depends on third-party hardware
Single-market reach

competitors.name: WerkDone Elder Care Management
competitors.pricing_range_published: Tiered SaaS — SGD 8–25/bed/mo (per WerkDone product page 2025)
competitors.primary_value_prop: Singapore-built elder-care management platform with care planning and reporting.
strengths: SG-incorporated; aligned to AIC + MOH templates
Local-language support and multi-tenant pricing
Mobile-first carer UX is rare in the SG aged-care segment
weaknesses: Workflow software only — no sensing layer
Tiny brand and team
No clinical-validation publications

website_design_notes.verdict: SG SME-template site: WordPress-era theme, gradient-heavy hero, mismatched icon styles (some flat, some 3D), stock 'happy elderly couple' photography. Coherence is the rubric leader here — the site does say what they sell (elder care management software) — but Modernness, Attractiveness, and Accessibility all sit at or below 3 due to low-contrast grey body text, missing alt attributes, and a mobile menu that breaks at 200% zoom. Screenshots pending.
website_design_notes.lumana_should_steal: Honestly, very little — but the explicit MOH/AIC eldercare-scheme references in the footer are a localisation cue Lumana should mirror for SG-targeted pages (PSG, SkillsFuture, AIC partner badges).
website_design_notes.lumana_should_reject: Reject the 'SG SaaS template' aesthetic wholesale: gradient hero buttons, smiling-elderly stock photos, and PowerPoint-flat feature icons signal 'low-budget agency build' to the same procurement officers Lumana needs to win against MNC-tier incumbents.
